actions.thought: Jack-Four offsuit is a very weak starting hand with poor playability and low equity against typical opponent ranges. Playing this hand would likely result in being dominated or facing difficult post-flop decisions. Folding is the most disciplined and profitable action to preserve my stack early in the tournament.

I am in the Small Blind with 7d 3s, which is an extremely weak starting hand. The Button has folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind. While I only need to call 50 more chips to see a flop, 73o has very poor playability and I would be out of position for the remainder of the hand. In a 3-handed game, I should be selective, and 73o is near the bottom of my range. Folding is the safest and most standard play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am on the button with 6-4 offsuit. While I have positional advantage, 6-4o is a very weak starting hand and generally does not perform well in a 3-handed game, even for a steal attempt. With 100BB stacks, I prefer to wait for better opportunities rather than risking chips with a bottom-tier hand.

Pocket aces are the premium pre-flop holding. I am opening from the Small Blind after the Button folded. I will make a standard raise to 300 (3x the big blind) to build the pot while keeping the Big Blind's calling range reasonably wide.

I am in the big blind with Queen-Six offsuit. This is a weak hand with poor playability, especially when playing out of position against a raise from the small blind. While the pot odds are decent, the long-term disadvantage of playing this range out of position suggests that folding is the most disciplined and +EV strategy this early in the tournament.

In a 3-handed game, the Button open range is very wide. K9o is certainly strong enough to open. I will make a min-raise to 200 to put pressure on the blinds and attempt to steal them while keeping my risk low.

K3o is a weak, unsuited holding that plays poorly out of position. Defending the small blind against a button open with this hand leads to reverse implied odds and difficult post-flop spots. Folding is the most disciplined and long-term profitable decision to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Five offsuit. The Button (Pro) has made a minimum raise to 200. While the pot odds are tempting (calling 100 to win a total pot of 450), J5o is a very weak hand that performs poorly out of position. Defending such a wide range this early in the tournament against an active player is likely to lead to difficult post-flop situations. I will play it safe and fold.

J3 offsuit is a very weak starting hand, especially in a 3-handed game where position provides some value but not enough to overcome the poor quality of the cards. I will fold to preserve my stack and wait for a more playable hand.

I am in the Small Blind with 7h 8s. After the Button folds, it is just me and the Big Blind (Pro). 78o is a reasonable hand to play in a heads-up situation from the blinds. I will raise to 300 to put pressure on the Big Blind and potentially take down the blinds now, or play a pot with a hand that has some connectivity if called.

K3o is a very weak starting hand. Facing a 3x raise from the small blind, even though we have positional advantage post-flop, the hand is easily dominated and has poor playability. Folding is the standard and most profitable decision to preserve our stack.

I am on the button with a suited King (Kh 6h). In a 3-handed game, this is a clear opening hand. Raising to 300 (3x the big blind) allows me to take the initiative and potentially win the blinds or play the rest of the hand in position against the Small or Big Blind.

T6s is a weak hand to play out of position against a 3x open raise from the button. Even though it is suited, it has poor playability and is often dominated. Folding is the most standard and EV-maximizing choice here.

I am in the Big Blind with 9d-2s, which is an extremely weak hand with no equity or playability. While I am getting decent pot odds (200 to win 450), defending with this range is mathematically poor as I will be out of position post-flop with a dominated hand. It is better to preserve my stack and wait for a more favorable situation.
Holding 3s 5d on the Button is a very weak, unsuited, disconnected hand. The most profitable play over the long run with this holding is to fold pre-flop and wait for better opportunities.

I am in the small blind and the button folded. I have Queen-Nine offsuit, which is a marginal hand, but it is playable in a heads-up situation against the big blind. Completing the small blind to see a flop is a standard, low-risk move given the pot odds and the fact that I am in position relative to the big blind post-flop. Calling 50 to complete the total commitment to 100 is appropriate.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten of Spades and Five of Hearts. The Button (Pro) folded, and the Small Blind (Flashlight) just completed the bet by calling the extra 50 chips. Since the pot is already matched and I have a weak starting hand (T5o), there is no reason to raise. I will check to see a free flop and evaluate the hand based on the board texture.

The flop is Ace-high, which is likely to have hit the opponent's range given they checked the big blind pre-flop. With Queen-high and only a backdoor flush draw, I have minimal equity to barrel here. Checking allows me to see the turn for free while keeping the pot small and minimizing risk against a potential Ace in my opponent's range.

I have air on an Ace-high board with potential straight and flush draws. Since Flashlight checked to me in this limped pot, there is no reason to bet with Ten-high. I will check back to see a free turn card and evaluate if my hand improves or if the board texture changes significantly.

The board is paired with 6s and 6d, and both players checked the flop, suggesting a lack of strength from both sides. My Queen-high has no showdown value and minimal equity to improve. Betting here as a bluff is risky as it could easily run into an Ace or a 7. Checking is the most sound strategic choice to keep the pot size controlled and see the river for free.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-high on a board of Ah-6s-7h-6d. The pot was limped pre-flop and checked through on the flop. Flashlight has checked again on the turn. While I could attempt a bluff, there is very little incentive to do so in such a small pot with no real equity. I will check back to see the river for free and potentially win at showdown if the board runs out in a way that favors my Ten-high or if I happen to improve.

The board is A-6-7-6-3. My hand is Queen-high, which loses to any pair, including a pair of threes or sixes, and certainly any Ace. Since my opponent checked down the previous streets, they might have a weak hand, but they are still likely to have me beaten with any pair. Betting here would be a pure bluff that is unlikely to force a fold from any reasonable holding in the big blind's check-down range. Checking is the most logical play to minimize losses.

The hand has been checked down on every street in a limped pot. I hold Ten-high, which has very limited showdown value. However, in a 200-chip pot that has shown no aggression from my opponent, there is no reason to risk chips on a bluff. I will check and see the showdown.

Queen-six offsuit is a weak holding that lacks significant playability or high-card strength. Despite being on the button, there is no advantage to entering the pot with such a marginal hand given the stack depth. It is better to conserve my stack and wait for a more premium hand to engage in the action.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Ten offsuit. Flashlight on the Button has folded, making this a heads-up situation against Pro in the Big Blind. QTo is a strong hand to open for a raise in a blind-vs-blind battle. Raising allows me to take the initiative and potentially win the blinds immediately or put pressure on the Big Blind post-flop. I will raise to 300, which is a standard 3x open.

Facing a 3x open raise from the small blind, my hand (Ten-Eight offsuit) is too weak to defend profitably. While the small blind might be opening wide to steal the big blind, T8o lacks the playability and equity needed to justify a call or a 3-bet. Folding is the optimal decision.

Holding Ace-Five offsuit on the button in a three-handed game is a strong opening hand. I have the advantage of position and want to put pressure on the blinds. My previous standard opening raise has been 300, and I will maintain that sizing to remain consistent and build a pot with a hand that has decent equity and blockers.

K8 suited is a marginal hand to play from the Small Blind facing a 3x raise from the Button. While it has some flush and straight potential, playing a relatively weak King out of position often leads to being dominated or facing difficult post-flop decisions. Folding is the safest and most standard play here.

K7o is a weak hand to defend from the Big Blind, especially against a 3x button open. Playing out of position against an aggressive opponent like 'Flash' makes it difficult to realize equity post-flop, and the hand is likely dominated. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve the stack early in the tournament.

76o is a very marginal and weak hand, even when opening from the Button in a 3-handed game. With two players left to act and full 100BB stacks, opening this offsuit connector has negative expected value. Folding is the best play to preserve our stack for better opportunities.

I am in the small blind heads-up against the big blind after the button folded. I have a pocket pair (5s, 5c), which is a strong hand in this situation. Raising is the optimal move here to take the betting initiative, put pressure on the big blind, and potentially win the pot pre-flop or build value for a favorable post-flop scenario.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Five offsuit. The Small Blind has raised to 300 (a 3x open). While I have position for the rest of the hand, Q5o is a very weak starting hand that doesn't flop well. Given the stack depths and the size of the raise, it is more profitable to fold here and wait for a better spot than to defend with a marginal hand that is likely dominated by the Small Blind's raising range.

With 8-10 offsuit, I have a marginal starting hand with poor connectivity and low showdown value. As the first player to act in a 3-handed game on the button, there is no need to play a weak hand that will likely lead to difficult post-flop decisions. Preserving my stack for more profitable situations is the optimal strategy.

I have a premium hand with Ace-King suited (Ks As). After the button folded, I am in a heads-up situation against the Big Blind. I will raise to build the pot and take the initiative, staying consistent with my previous pre-flop sizing of 300 chips.

Holding pocket fives in the Big Blind facing a Small Blind open is a standard defend. We will have position post-flop and excellent implied odds to hit a set. Calling allows us to keep the pot manageable and easily realize our equity.

On this flop of 8c 2h 9c, I have two overcards with Ace-King of Spades. While the board texture is somewhat connected and can hit the Big Blind's defending range, I still hold the range advantage as the pre-flop aggressor. I will place a continuation bet of 300 (half the pot) to maintain initiative, fold out weaker hands, and extract value from worse high-card hands or draws.

We hold pocket fives on an 8-2-9 flop with two clubs. The opponent c-bets half pot. Our hand is an underpair to the two top cards on the board, and we have very few outs to improve to a set. The board is quite wet, coordinating with many hands in the opponent's pre-flop raising range such as overcards with flush draws, straight draws, and higher pairs. Calling here will put us in a tough spot on most turn cards if we face continued aggression. Folding is the most disciplined and optimal play.

I am on the Button with King-Nine suited, which is a strong starting hand in a 3-handed game. I have been playing an aggressive style, and 3x raises have been effective or standard at this table. Raising here allows me to take control of the pot, put pressure on the blinds, and leverage my positional advantage throughout the hand.

I am dealt Ace-King offsuit in the Small Blind facing a Button open from Flash, who has been opening frequently to 300. AKo is a premium hand that is far ahead of a standard Button opening range. I want to 3-bet to extract value, take the betting initiative, and potentially win the pot right now, so I will raise to 1000.

I am in the Big Blind with one of the worst hands in poker, 3-2 offsuit. After an open raise and a 3-bet, it is mathematically incorrect and strategically unsound to continue. I must fold and preserve my stack for a better spot.

I opened from the button with King-Nine of spades, a strong suited king in a 3-handed game. Pro responded with a 3-bet to 1000 from the small blind. Given the positional advantage I hold and the playability of suited connectors/kings in shorthanded play, folding would be too tight. The odds are roughly 2:1 to call, and I have the stack depth to play post-flop. I will call the 3-bet to see a flop and leverage my position.

I have top pair, top kicker on a relatively dry flop of Ad 8c 7s in a 3-bet pot. Being out of position against Flash, I want to continuation bet for value to target worse Aces like AQ, AJ, AT, as well as occasional straight draws or floating pocket pairs. A bet of around 1/3 pot (700 chips) is a standard sizing that extracts value while keeping his continuing range wide.

The flop of Ad-8c-7s is very favorable for the pre-flop 3-bettor's range, which likely includes many Ace-x combinations and high pocket pairs. I missed the board entirely with King-Nine of spades, holding only King-high with no significant draws. While the 700 bet from Pro is small relative to the pot (1/3), I have very little equity and limited ways to improve to the best hand. Floating here against a strong player on an Ace-high board is risky, and folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack.
I am on the Button with an unplayable offsuit hand (Jack-Six offsuit). The standard and optimal action is to fold and wait for a better starting hand.

With the button folding, I am now heads-up against the big blind. King-eight offsuit is a strong enough holding in this position to take the initiative. By raising to 300, I can potentially win the blinds immediately or take control of the hand going into the flop.

I am in the Big Blind with 8h 9h, a strong suited connector. Flashlight (SB) raised to 300, and Pro folded. I need to call 200 more into a pot of 400, giving me 3:1 pot odds. Suited connectors like 89s play very well post-flop, especially against a small blind opening range which is typically wide. I will call to see the flop.

I have flopped top pair on a board that contains several draws, including flush and straight possibilities. With the King of Clubs, I have a decent blocker, but my kicker is weak, so I want to charge draws and protect my equity. Betting half the pot (300) is a standard continuation bet to define the opponent's range and maintain initiative.

On a board of Ts-Kh-9s, I have bottom pair (9s) with a weak kicker. Flashlight, the pre-flop aggressor from the small blind, has placed a continuation bet of 300 into a 600 pot. This is a half-pot bet. I am getting 3:1 pot odds to call. While my hand is relatively weak on this coordinated board, it is too strong to fold immediately in a blind-vs-blind battle against a single bet. I also have a backdoor heart flush draw and a very remote straight possibility. I will call this bet to see how the turn develops, but I will remain cautious if the action continues to be aggressive.

I hold top pair with a King. The turn card, the 2 of Clubs, is a relatively safe card as it does not complete the obvious flush or straight draws present on the flop. Given the pot size and the board texture, I want to continue to build the pot and charge any drawing hands for their next card. Betting half the pot (600) keeps the initiative and provides a balance between value and protection.

I have bottom pair (nines) on a highly coordinated board (Ts-Kh-9s-2c). Flashlight has shown consistent aggression, betting both the flop and the turn. Given the presence of many overcards (King, Ten) and the high likelihood of straights or better pairs in the opponent's range, my hand is likely behind. Calling 600 chips here into an 1800 pot is marginal, and I will face a very difficult decision on the river if Flashlight continues betting. To preserve my stack for better opportunities, I will fold.

I am in the button position with Queen-Ten offsuit. This is a solid hand to play in a three-handed game, and being on the button gives me a significant positional advantage. I will open to 300 to attempt to take down the blinds or gain the initiative.

I have AKo, which is a premium hand in a 3-handed game. Against a button open of 3x, a 3-bet is standard for value and to gain initiative while out of position. I'll raise to 1000 to put pressure on the opener and potentially isolate.

With Q9 offsuit facing an open from the Button and a 3-bet from the Small Blind, this is an easy fold. The hand is weak, easily dominated, and out of position against the initial raiser. Folding and conserving my stack is the only correct play here.

Facing a 3-bet from the small blind with Queen-Ten offsuit, I am likely behind the opponent's 3-bet range. While I have the advantage of being on the button, the size of the 3-bet makes calling a difficult proposition, as my hand is easily dominated by stronger broadway combinations. Given that we are early in the tournament, I prefer to conserve my stack and wait for a more favorable spot to engage.

I am on the button with Ace-Ten offsuit, which is a strong starting hand in a 3-handed game. I will make a standard opening raise to 3x the big blind (300 chips) to exert pressure on the blinds and take control of the hand in position. This sizing has been my consistent opening raise throughout the tournament.
5d4h is a weak, offsuit, disconnected hand that plays very poorly out of position. Facing a 3x raise from the button, the best and most standard move is to simply fold and wait for a better spot.

I hold pocket Queens, which is a premium hand. Facing a raise from the button, my optimal strategy is to 3-bet. This allows me to take control of the hand, build the pot, and capitalize on my equity. A raise to 1000 is an appropriate sizing to define the range of my opponent and potentially induce a call or a 4-bet, while maintaining a strong position.

I opened to 300 with Ace-Ten offsuit from the button, a strong hand for three-handed play. The big blind (Flashlight) 3-bet to 1000. While A-To is vulnerable to a 3-bet, it is too strong to fold in a 3-handed game given my positional advantage. I will call to see a flop and use my position to navigate the rest of the hand.

The flop is unfavorable as it contains an Ace, which likely hits my opponent's 3-bet calling range, especially since I hold pocket Queens. The board is also relatively wet with a flush draw present. As the out-of-position player, I should check to control the pot size and avoid inflating it against a range that dominates mine. This allows me to see if my opponent checks behind or dictates the price of the hand.

On a board of Ad-Jd-6s, I have top pair with a Ten kicker. Flashlight, who 3-bet pre-flop, has checked to me. While I have a strong hand, checking back here serves two purposes: first, it controls the size of the pot with a hand that might be outkicked by AK or AJ; second, it protects my checking range and allows me to see a turn card for free. If Flashlight was trapping with a monster or has a draw, checking back keeps the pot manageable and allows me to evaluate the action on the turn.

The board has become highly coordinated with an Ace, King, and Jack. My pocket Queens are now essentially a bluff-catcher, as any Ace, King, or even Jack in my opponent's range beats me. Since my opponent checked back the flop, they might be trap-playing an Ace or simply have a weak hand, but betting into this board structure does not provide value as I will only get called by better hands. Checking allows me to control the pot size and navigate towards a cheap showdown.

I have top pair with a Ten kicker on a board containing Ace, King, and Jack. Flashlight, the pre-flop 3-bettor, has checked for the second time. While my hand is strong, the board is very coordinated and many hands in a 3-bet range (AK, AJ, AQ) have me beat. By checking back again, I control the pot size and move towards a cheap showdown, while also avoiding a potential check-raise. If Flashlight has a hand like QQ or a missed draw, they are unlikely to call a bet anyway, so checking back allows me to realize my showdown value.

The board (A-K-J-6-3) is quite dangerous for pocket Queens, as any Ace or King in the opponent's range beats me. Since both the flop and turn were checked through by both players, it is unlikely my opponent has a monster hand, but they likely have a marginal holding or a missed draw. Betting now is suboptimal as it will likely only be called by hands that beat me (like a pair of Aces or Kings), while I will fold out hands that I already beat. Therefore, checking to reach a showdown is the most prudent strategy to capitalize on my relative hand strength against potential missed draws or smaller pairs.

I have top pair with an Ace and a Ten kicker. Given the action, Flashlight (the pre-flop 3-bettor) has checked on every street (flop, turn, and river). While I likely have the best hand against a range that includes missed draws or smaller pocket pairs, the board is quite coordinated with an Ace, King, and Jack. If I bet, I am unlikely to get called by worse hands, and I risk being check-raised by a stronger hand that was trapping. Checking back allows me to realize my showdown value and win the pot without further risk.

With pocket fives on the Button in a 3-handed game, we have a decent starting hand. An open-raise to 3 times the big blind (300) is standard to steal the blinds or take control of the hand going to the flop. If we get called, we have position and the potential to flop a set.

Holding Jack-Three offsuit is a bottom-tier hand, especially when out of position in the small blind against a button open-raise. There is no strategic value in defending this hand as it has poor equity and will be difficult to play post-flop. Folding is the correct mathematical and strategic decision to preserve the stack for better opportunities.

I am in the Big Blind holding Ace-Deuce of clubs. The Button has raised to 300. A2s is a strong hand to defend against a button open, offering nut-flush potential and decent playability. Since I am out of position, I will call to see a flop and keep the pot controlled rather than bloating it with a 3-bet.

I am the Big Blind and the pre-flop action was a raise from Pro on the Button. The flop is 4s-3s-6d, and I have Ace-Deuce of clubs. This gives me a gutshot straight draw (needing any 5 for a straight) and one overcard (the Ace). Against the pre-flop aggressor, the standard play out of position is to check and see how they react. I want to see a cheap turn card to potentially hit my straight or improve my hand.

We have an open-ended straight draw and a middle pair on a wet flop with two spades. While betting for protection or as a semi-bluff is an option, checking behind is usually safer here. It controls the size of the pot and avoids a potential check-raise that could force us to fold a high-equity hand. Taking a free card guarantees we see the turn and realize our equity.

On the turn, the Queen of Diamonds appears. Since Pro checked back on the low flop, their range likely consists of mid-to-low pocket pairs, overcards, or perhaps they are pot-controlling a monster. My hand (Ace-high with a gutshot) has some showdown value against their total air and some equity to improve on the river. Leading out now would turn my hand into a bluff, which isn't necessary given that I might win at showdown or hit my straight for free. Checking is the most prudent play to see the river card or evaluate a potential bet from Pro.

Flash has checked twice, showing significant weakness. We have a pair of fives and an open-ended straight draw. Betting here serves as a semi-bluff and protection, allowing us to deny equity to overcards and draws while potentially folding out stronger A-highs. If called, we still have up to 10 outs to improve to a strong hand on the river.

Pro's delayed continuation bet on the turn (betting after checking the flop) is a strong move that often represents either a Queen that just hit or a medium pair that was pot-controlling. My hand, Ace-Deuce, currently only has Ace-high showdown value and a gutshot straight draw (needing a 5). Mathematically, calling 350 to win a total pot of 1350 requires approximately 26% equity. With only 4 outs to a straight (roughly 8.7% equity), I do not have the direct pot odds to call. Given that Ace-high is unlikely to win at showdown if Pro continues aggression on the river, and the implied odds of hitting a 5 are not certain enough to justify the call, folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack.

8-4 offsuit is a very weak hand with extremely poor playability and no real equity. Given that I am in the early stages of the tournament and there is no raise before me, the most disciplined and optimal strategy is to fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Small Blind with J7o. The Button (Flashlight) has folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind (Pro). J7o is a weak hand, but in a blind-versus-blind scenario, it's often worth playing to steal the blinds or defend. However, Pro has been playing relatively aggressively and my hand doesn't play particularly well post-flop if I just call. Folding is a very safe option to preserve my stack, but calling is also an option given the pot odds (50 to win 150). Given the dynamics and my position, I will fold and wait for a better spot, as J7o is near the bottom of my range here.

I am on the button with Ten of Clubs and Eight of Clubs, which is a solid hand to open in a three-handed game. Raising here allows me to take control of the pot and potentially win the blinds or play the hand in position against the small or big blind. A standard raise to 300 has been my consistent opening size and is effective here.

I am in the Small Blind with 5c 9d, which is a very weak, unsuited, and disconnected hand. Facing a 3bb raise from the Button, I have terrible pot odds and poor playability out of position. This is an easy fold.

Facing a 300 raise from the button, I need to call 200 to see the flop. With Queen-Five offsuit, I have reasonable equity to defend the big blind against a likely wide button opening range. Getting 2.25:1 pot odds makes this a profitable call to see a flop in position.

I have top pair with a Queen on a dry, rainbow board of Qd-2s-8h. As the out-of-position player who called the pre-flop raise, the standard play is to check to the aggressor (the button) and evaluate their continuation bet. I intend to proceed cautiously given my weak kicker, but top pair is certainly strong enough to check-call against a reasonable continuation bet.

On a dry board of Qd 2s 8h, I have middle pair (8s) with a 10 kicker. Flashlight (Big Blind) checked to me after calling pre-flop. While a continuation bet is an option, middle pair is a classic hand for pot control and checking back in position. This allows me to keep the pot small, protect my checking range, and see a turn card for free. There are few draws to worry about, so I'm not giving away too much equity by checking.

I am in the Big Blind with a pair of Queens on a board of Qd-2s-8h-Kd. Since the opponent (Flash) checked behind on the flop, their range likely lacks strong hands like sets or top pairs, or they are playing cautiously. The King on the turn improves the board, making my top pair with a weak kicker more vulnerable. Betting here is unlikely to get value from worse hands and would only bloat the pot when I am likely behind a King or Ace-high. Checking is the standard play to control the pot size, maintain showdown value, and see if the river provides a safe conclusion to the hand.

I have a pair of eights on a board showing Qd-2s-8h-Kd. Flashlight (Big Blind) has checked for the second time. My hand has decent showdown value but is unlikely to be called by many worse hands if I bet. By checking back, I control the size of the pot and move to the river, where I can evaluate Flashlight's final action. This line protects my range and allows me to reach showdown cheaply with a marginal made hand.

I am in the Big Blind with a pair of Queens (Qs, Qd). The board is Qd-2s-8h-Kd-Ts. The opponent (Flash) has checked behind on both the flop and the turn, which suggests they do not have a strong hand like a set, two pair, or a King. However, with my weak kicker (5), a bet here is unlikely to get value from worse hands and could potentially fold out hands I can beat while risking a check-raise. Checking is the most prudent play to reach a showdown and evaluate my hand's strength, as the pair of Queens is still a solid holding in a checked-down pot.

On the river, the Ten of Spades arrives, improving my hand to two pair (Tens and Eights). While this is a strong hand in a pot that has been checked all the way, the board is quite coordinated with a Queen, King, and Ten. Any Jack now completes a straight (9-10-J-Q-K), and any King or Queen beats me. Flashlight has checked for the third time, which often indicates a weak hand or a missed draw, but could also be a trap with a marginal made hand or a slow-played monster. Given that a bet is unlikely to be called by many worse hands and could be raised by better ones, checking back is the safest way to realize my showdown value and take down the pot.
Q4o is a very weak hand and generally unplayable from the button. There is no strategic reason to try and steal the blinds here, as the risk outweighs the reward. Folding is the optimal play.

I am in the Small Blind position and the Button (Pro) has folded, leaving me heads-up against the Big Blind (Flash). My hand, Ace-Deuce offsuit, is a strong enough starting hand to contest the pot against one opponent. I will raise to 300 to take the initiative and pressure the Big Blind, as Ace-high is likely the best hand currently.

I am in the Big Blind holding Ace-Queen offsuit, which is a premium hand in a 3-handed game. The Small Blind (Flashlight) has opened with a raise to 300. In a blind-versus-blind scenario, ranges are typically very wide, and AQo is well ahead of Flashlight's likely opening range. I have the advantage of being in position for the rest of the hand. I will 3-bet (re-raise) for value to build the pot and take control of the hand. A raise to 900 is a standard size (3x the open) that applies pressure and extracts value from weaker Aces and Kings.

Facing a 3-bet from the Big Blind after I opened from the Small Blind puts me in a difficult position out of position with a weak Ace. Ace-Deuce offsuit is easily dominated by the opponent's 3-betting range, and calling would result in playing a bloated pot without initiative. Folding is the disciplined, low-variance play to preserve my stack.

As the button in a three-handed game, I have the positional advantage and should look to open the pot to steal the blinds or gain initiative. Ace-high is a strong enough starting hand to open with, even with an offsuit deuce, so I will make a standard raise of 3x the big blind.

I am in the Small Blind with 6s 7d. Flashlight has raised to 3x from the Button. While 76o has some connectivity, it is a very weak hand to play out of position, especially with a chip leader (Pro) still to act in the Big Blind. Calling would put me in a difficult spot post-flop against one or two opponents. Given the tournament context and my current stack, there's no need to defend with such a marginal hand.

I am facing a button raise from Flashlight while in the big blind with a low-to-medium pocket pair (6h 6c). Calling is the standard and most profitable play here, as it allows me to set-mine with excellent implied odds given the deep effective stacks. If I hit a set on the flop, I can potentially win a large pot; if I miss and face aggression on unfavorable textures, I can let the hand go.

I missed my set on a board that contains two overcards to my pair of sixes (Ten and Nine). The standard play here as the pre-flop caller out of position is to check to the pre-flop aggressor and evaluate their action. On the river, the board is 6s 3s Qh 2s 8h. I hold Ad 3d, giving me bottom pair. I am on the button with Ace-Five suited, which is a strong enough hand to open in a three-handed game. Holding J2o in the Small Blind facing a Button raise is a clear fold. The hand is extremely weak and plays terribly out of position, offering virtually no playability or equity to justify calling or 3-betting.

I have improved to a pair of fives on the turn, but it is a weak pair on a board with several potential connectors and overcards. I am in the small blind and the button has folded. With K6o, I have a marginal hand but it is playable heads-up against the big blind. Calling to see a flop is a standard, low-risk approach here to exercise position post-flop without bloating the pot with a weak holding.

I am in the big blind with a weak hand (8c 5h). The small blind has limped, and the pot is now 200 chips. There is no reason to bluff or build a pot with this hand, so I will take the free flop and check.

I have middle pair (eights) on an Ace-high board in a limped pot. Flashlight checked to me, and since the pot is small and there is an Ace present, I have some showdown value but not enough to bet for value. Checking back allows me to see the turn for free and keep the pot manageable.

In this limped pot, I have middle pair (Eights) on a board that now shows three diamonds and an Ace. My hand (King high) has no showdown value against an opponent who called a turn bet on this board. The board contains an Ace and is paired, making it extremely unlikely my King high is good. Bluffing here would be unprofitable as the opponent showed strength by calling the turn, indicating they likely have at least a piece of the board or a hand they are unwilling to fold. In this limped pot, I have a pair of eights. The board is quite dangerous with an Ace and three diamonds, but Flashlight's check on the river suggests they might not have the flush or a strong Ace. Holding 3d-4h is a very weak hand with low strategic value. Given that the current stack is 8700 and there is no strong reason to contest the pot with such poor cards, the most disciplined and profitable play is to fold and preserve the stack for a better opportunity.

Folded to the small blind. I'm holding suited connectors (4d 6d). While the hand is low, it has decent playability and provides a good opportunity to put pressure on the big blind. I am currently the chip leader with a significant advantage. While I am on the button and 3-handed play typically demands a wide opening range, 9-6 offsuit is near the bottom of my range. With King-Seven offsuit in the small blind and the button folding, this is a classic blind-versus-blind situation. K7o is easily strong enough to play, and raising allows me to take the initiative and apply pressure on the big blind, potentially winning the 150 pot outright without seeing a flop.

I hold 9d 7h in the big blind, which is a weak hand with poor playability. The small blind has raised to 300, and given the lack of equity and the disadvantage of being out of position, the most disciplined and +EV play is to fold and wait for a better spot.
With JTo on the button in a 3-handed game, this is a standard open-raise. I can often steal the blinds, and if I get called, I'll have position post-flop with a hand that can flop reasonably well.

I am in the small blind with a weak, disconnected hand (9h, 4c). Calling 250 chips out of position against a raise is mathematically unfavorable and puts me in a difficult spot post-flop. I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Nine suited, a premium hand in a 3-handed game. The Button (Pro) has raised to 300, which is a standard opening. I opened JTo from the Button, which is a standard steal attempt. However, facing a 3-bet from the Big Blind, JTo is simply too weak to continue. Calling would put too much of my stack at risk with a hand that is easily dominated by the BB's 3-betting range (like AJ, KJ, AT, or overpairs). Folding to preserve my stack is the best play.

My hand, Ten of Hearts and Six of Hearts, is a weak suited connector that does not play well in a three-handed game. I am in the Small Blind with King-Trey offsuit. The Button (Flashlight) has folded, leaving me heads-up against the Big Blind (Pro). Q3o is a very weak offsuit hand. Facing a 3x raise from the Small Blind, folding is the standard and correct decision to preserve our stack.

I am on the button with Ten-Deuce offsuit. I am on the Button with Jack-Deuce offsuit, which is a very weak and unplayable starting hand. The standard and optimal play here is to fold and wait for a better spot to invest chips.

I am in the Small Blind with a strong hand, King-Queen suited, and the Button has folded. This is a favorable spot to take the initiative against the Big Blind. Raising to 300 is a standard sizing that aligns with the table meta and allows me to build a pot with my premium holding.

I am in the Big Blind with a premium hand, Ace-Queen offsuit, in a blind-versus-blind situation. Flashlight has raised to 300 from the Small Blind. I am in the Small Blind holding King-Queen suited. After I opened to 300, the Big Blind (Flash) 3-bet to 1000. Flash has been aggressive throughout the tournament, and KQs is a strong hand that plays well in position or even out of position. Folding is not an option. I missed the flop completely with my King-Queen suited. The board of 8-J-6 is uncoordinated but hits the likely 3-betting range of my opponent (Flash) well. I am the pre-flop aggressor in a 3-bet pot and have a significant range advantage. The board of 8-J-6-9 is extremely coordinated, and my King-Queen suited has failed to connect with any of the cards. I am in the Small Blind holding King-Queen offsuit on a board of 8-J-6-9-4. I have no pair and no showdown value, only King-High. With Q3 offsuit in the big blind, I have a very weak hand with poor playability out of position. Since both opponents just limped, I can check and see a free flop without risking any more chips. Raising as a bluff here multi-way is unnecessary and unprofitable.

I am first to act on the flop with 6-high and no real draw on an Ace-high board. I have a pair of nines on an Ace-high board, which is a very marginal holding. Since both opponents checked to me, they are likely either weak or trapping. With Q-high and a weak gutshot straight draw (needing a 2) on a board of 9-A-4-5, my hand has very little equity. Both opponents checked the flop, and the Small Blind has checked again on the turn. The board is Nine-high on the turn with an Ace, Four, and Five. I hold a pair of nines, which is a marginal hand that has some showdown value but is vulnerable. On the river, the board is 9c-Ah-4c-5d-Js. I have a pair of fives. With Queen-high on an Ace-high board that just completed a Jack, I have no showdown value. The pot is small, and attempting a multi-way bluff here with zero equity if called is an unnecessary risk. The pot was limped pre-flop and checked through on the flop and turn, indicating that no player has a particularly strong hand. Given the passive nature of the hand so far, it is highly possible Flashlight is bluffing with air or a lower pair. I will call to see the showdown.

With Queen-high, I have absolutely no chance of winning at showdown. The button bet 200 and the small blind called 200, so there are two players showing interest in the pot. Folding is the only logical choice here.

K8o is too weak of a hand to play from the Small Blind against a Button open, especially being out of position and with the Big Blind still left to act. The standard and most profitable play here is to fold.

I am in the big blind with ten-high (Ts-7d) on a Jack-high, coordinated, but essentially dry board (Js-3s-4h). My holding, Seven-Three offsuit, is extremely weak and offers poor playability. Calling or raising out of position against a button raise with such a hand would be negative expected value. Therefore, folding is the correct and disciplined strategic decision.

My hand is 2-8 offsuit, which is extremely weak and has very little equity. Even though I am on the button, there is no strategic justification to enter the pot with such a poor holding. Folding is the disciplined play to conserve my stack for more profitable opportunities.

Q3o is a very weak, disconnected offsuit hand. From the Button, this falls well outside standard opening ranges. Since my stack is still relatively healthy at over 60 big blinds, there is no need to try and make a marginal steal with such poor holdings. The correct play is to fold.

Ten-six offsuit is a very weak hand, especially out of position in a blind battle against the chip leader. It is better to conserve my stack and fold rather than playing a marginal hand out of position.
7-4 offsuit is one of the worst starting hands in No-Limit Texas Hold'em. With no playable equity and no incentive to play a weak hand, the only sound strategic move is to fold.

I am in the Big Blind with 5s 3c, which is a very weak offsuit connector. Facing a raise to 300 from the Small Blind, this hand has poor equity and very little playability post-flop. Folding is the standard and correct play.

I am on the button with Ten-Trey offsuit, which is a very weak hand. While I have a significant chip lead and could potentially steal the blinds, T3o is near the bottom of the starting hand range. The small blind (Pro) and big blind (Flashlight) have shown they are willing to defend or 3-bet in previous hands. I am holding 5c-9h, which is a very weak, uncoordinated, off-suit hand. On the button 3-handed, Jack-Ten suited is a strong starting hand that plays very well post-flop. An open raise to 3 big blinds is standard to steal the blinds or build a pot in position with a very playable hand.

With Ace-Seven offsuit in the small blind, I am facing a standard raise from the button. While I am out of position, the hand strength is sufficient to justify a call given the pot odds (calling 250 to win a pot of 450). Folding here would be too conservative, and 3-betting out of position against an aggressive button is higher variance than I want at this stage.

I am in the big blind with 7h 8c. Pro raised to 300 from the button, and Flashlight called from the small blind. I need to put in 200 more to call. The pot is currently 700, giving me 3.5:1 odds. Given these excellent pot odds and my significant chip lead, I will call to see the flop in this three-way pot, closing the action pre-flop.

I have top pair with an Ace on a relatively dry board. I have 7-high on an Ace-high board in a three-way pot. I have no pair and no real draw. Flashlight has already checked. Since I am out of position and have no equity in this hand, I will check to see what the pre-flop aggressor (Pro) does. I intend to fold if any significant betting action occurs.

I completely missed the flop in a 3-way pot. With just Jack-high, no direct draws, and two opponents who could easily have connected with the Ace or hold a flush draw, checking to see a free card is the safest play. C-betting as a pure bluff with virtually no equity against multiple players is too risky here.
